title: Snake the Deep Drop
game_context: 5-on-5 half-court. You are the point guard initiating a high ball-screen at the top of the key. Your screener (5) sets a clean screen on your right. The screen defender (x5) is coached to drop on every ball-screen, but on this rep he drops *deeper* than D1 — his chest is at the elbow, not the free-throw line, and his feet are at the lane mark.
possession_setup: Top-of-the-key high pick-and-roll. The screen comes from the right. x5 reads the ball-screen but drops two extra steps lower than a textbook drop — chest below the elbow, feet pointing at his own rim, eyes locked on the ball-handler. The on-ball defender (x1) is fighting *over* the screen, chasing your hip. The other three defenders are shell-honest.
decision_moment: You come off the screen and the pocket looks open — but x5's drop is so deep that the middle of the floor is wide open behind him. The on-ball defender is over the screen, chasing on your hip. Snake the dribble back across the screen and attack the now-vacated middle.
visible_cue: x5's chest is below the elbow — clear deep-drop posture. His feet point back at his own rim. His vision cone is locked on you (the ball-handler), not the roller. The middle of the floor behind him is empty because his drop conceded it.
prompt: You turn the corner. The big has dropped two steps lower than a textbook drop. What's the read?

choices.label: Snake the dribble back across the screen — attack the middle.
choices.feedback_text: Best read. The deep drop conceded the middle behind it. Cross back across the screen and attack the open paint while x1 is still trailing.

choices.label: Pull up in the pocket.
choices.feedback_text: Okay. The pocket is still there, but a deep drop opens the middle wider than the pocket — snake is the bigger advantage.
choices.partial_feedback_text: Pull-up works against a settled D1 drop. This big is deeper — the middle is the better take.

choices.label: Drive at x5's chest into the paint.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Driving straight at a deep-drop big walks you into his lap. The opening is behind him, not at him.

choices.label: Reset the ball to the wing.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Reset wastes the advantage. A deep drop is even more open than a textbook drop — the middle is yours.

best_read: Snake the dribble back across the screen and attack the middle of the floor. x5's deep drop conceded the area behind him — his feet are at the lane mark and his chest is below the elbow. The on-ball defender is fighting over the screen on your original hip, so the moment you snake he is on the wrong side of the floor. The middle paint is yours before any tag can rotate over.
acceptable_reads: Pull up in the pocket — still open against the deep drop, but trades a paint attack for a contested mid-range, so worse EV than the snake.
bad_reads: Drive at x5's chest — walks into the deepest part of the drop and a stationary contest.
Reset to the wing — wastes the bigger advantage a deep drop just handed you.
common_miss_reason: Treating every drop the same. Players who anchored on the D1 pull-up never re-read the depth — they take the pocket on every drop and never see the middle the deep drop opens behind it.
why_best_read_works: A deep drop is a coverage trade: x5 surrenders the middle of the floor in exchange for keeping his body between the ball and the rim. The trade only works if the on-ball defender stays attached on your hip — when x1 fights *over* the screen, he gets stuck on your original side. Snaking back across the screen flips the chase: x1 is now behind you AND on the wrong side, x5 is in front of you but well below the action, and the middle of the floor is empty. The drive into that middle beats the help rotation by half a beat.
decoder_teaching_point: Read the depth, not just the call. A textbook drop hands you the pocket; a deep drop hands you the middle behind it. Chest below the elbow + on-ball over the screen = snake.

feedback.correct: Good read. The deeper the drop, the more middle there is — you snaked into it before help could rotate.
feedback.partial: Pull-up was open, but the snake was open wider. Read the depth before you settle.
feedback.wrong: Look at his chest *and* his feet. Below the elbow, lane mark — that's deeper than D1. The middle is the answer, not the pocket and not the paint.

self_review_checklist: Did I read x5's depth, not just the call?
Did I notice x1 was over the screen, not under?
Did I snake before the help could rotate to the middle?
Did I attack the middle on balance, not into the big's chest?

coach_validation.notes: Pack 2 (Phase γ) — DROP-02 base scenario. D2: deep-drop variant, snake the screen. Single-freeze (no beatSpec.secondBeat). Adds defender_vision_cone(target=ball_handler) to the cue cluster (intermediate cap=3) per DROP_DECODER_DESIGN.md §3.2. Coach validation low+approved per the doc's D2 spec.

source_research_basis: Standard PnR coverage taxonomy. Deep-drop snake is the canonical D2 progression from textbook drop pull-up — same coverage family, deeper geometry, different exploit. Taught at every level above middle school as the natural follow-up rep to D1.
explanation_md: **Read the Coverage — Deep Drop.** A textbook drop hands you the pull-up pocket. A *deep* drop is the same call run two steps lower — and the trade-off opens further: the deeper the drop, the wider the middle of the floor behind it. The cue is the same chest line and foot arrow as DROP-01, plus a new piece — the vision cone locked on you tells you he is committed to the ball-handler, not the roller, so the snake will not run into a stunt. **Read the depth. Snake the screen. Attack the middle.**

wrongDemos.caption: You pulled up in the pocket. Open shot, but the snake was wider.
You drove at x5's chest. The deep drop ate the drive.
You reset to the wing. The deep-drop middle closed before the ball moved again.
